coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from mature green tea leaves for label-free fluorescence sensing of chromium(VI)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Patra, Swagata
Golder, Animes K.
Uppaluri, Ramagopal V.S.
coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) has been established as a highly carcinogenic and mutagenic entity. Therefore, there has been a persistent demand for affordable, sensitive, and quick sensors to monitor the presence of Cr(VI) in aqueous systems. A straightforward, cost-efficient, and eco-friendly approach has been reported in this work in terms of blue-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs, 5.2 nm) being realized in a single-step hydrothermal process. For this purpose, fully grown green tea leaves as the main carbon source were deployed due to being cheap, and environmentally sustainable and readily accessible sources. These CDs exhibit good water solubility, photo-stability and photo-emission (maximal emission at 438 nm for 360 nm excitation wavelength), anti-interference, and low cytotoxicity. The article demonstrates a straightforward fluorescent test for the precise detection and determination of Cr(VI). This was realized through the construction of a non-luminous complex which led to fluorescence quenching via the inner filter effect (IFE). Accordingly, the system can be employed as a label-free nanosensor. Under optimal condition (pH 4 and 5 min reaction time), the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs exhibited a well-defined linear correlation in the 0.01–8 mg/L concentration range of Cr(VI). A linear equation represents the system sensor as F0/F = 0.2101x + 1.0016 (R2 = 0.99). The limit of detection (LOD) has been as minimal as 0.004 mg/L (s/k = 3). These findings infer CDs as a potential cost-effective nanosensor for Cr(VI) identification in tap water specimens.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Mature green tea leaves
Fluorescence sensing
Cr(VI) detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in tap water samples
Spectroscopic and microscopic analyses of CDs
Detection of Cr(VI) in tap water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Centre for the Environment, Department of Chemical Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thankfully acknowledge the Centre for the Environment, Department of Chemical Engineering, and Central Instruments Facility, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, India, for providing the necessary facilities for carrying out this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of green synthesized CDs (inset image: Optical image of CDs under the normal light) and (b) Fluorescence emission spectra at variant excitation wavelengths (270–400 nm) (inset image: Optical image of CDs under the 365 nm ultraviolet light).
(a) FETEM image, (b) Particle size analysis, (c) HRTEM micrograph, (d) SAED, (e) XRD pattern, and (f) FTIR spectrum of green synthesized CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum, and XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1 s, and (d) O1s of the synthesized CDs.

(a) Selective Cr(VI) detection potential of green synthesized CDs in terms of fluorescence spectra (inset shows images of the CDs in the existence of various metal ions under UV exposure), (b) CDs fluorescence emission spectra at varied concentrations of Cr(VI) (λ ex = 360 nm), (c) Fitness plot of fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) and Cr(VI) concentration, and (d) Insensitivity of fluorescence intensity of Cr(VI) aqueous systems spiked with additional metal ions.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of dispersed CDs in tap water system with variant Cr(VI) concentrations and, (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs (excitation and emission) and UV–Vis absorption spectra of the Cr(VI).
Graphical representation for the synthesis of CDs from mature green tea leaves.
CDs-based Cr(VI) sensing performance achieved in this work vis-à-vis the prior art.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from mature green tea leaves for label-free fluorescence sensing of chromium(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for the Environment, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Assam, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for the Environment, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Assam, INDIA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Assam, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Assam, INDIA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Centre for the Environment, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Assam, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) has been established as a highly carcinogenic and mutagenic entity. Therefore, there has been a persistent demand for affordable, sensitive, and quick sensors to monitor the presence of Cr(VI) in aqueous systems. A straightforward, cost-efficient, and eco-friendly approach has been reported in this work in terms of blue-fluorescent carbon dots (CDs, 5.2 nm) being realized in a single-step hydrothermal process. For this purpose, fully grown green tea leaves as the main carbon source were deployed due to being cheap, and environmentally sustainable and readily accessible sources. These CDs exhibit good water solubility, photo-stability and photo-emission (maximal emission at 438 nm for 360 nm excitation wavelength), anti-interference, and low cytotoxicity. The article demonstrates a straightforward fluorescent test for the precise detection and determination of Cr(VI). This was realized through the construction of a non-luminous complex which led to fluorescence quenching via the inner filter effect (IFE). Accordingly, the system can be employed as a label-free nanosensor. Under optimal condition (pH 4 and 5 min reaction time), the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs exhibited a well-defined linear correlation in the 0.01–8 mg/L concentration range of Cr(VI). A linear equation represents the system sensor as F0/F = 0.2101x + 1.0016 (R2 = 0.99). The limit of detection (LOD) has been as minimal as 0.004 mg/L (s/k = 3). These findings infer CDs as a potential cost-effective nanosensor for Cr(VI) identification in tap water specimens.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium is a widely employed alloying element and is commonly applied in numerous industries including leather tanning, electroplating, pigment and dye manufacturing, wood conservation, steel processing, etc. Unfortunately, these industrial processes result in the substantial release of chromium into natural water bodies. Thereby, they create a noteworthy hazard to human well-being as well as habitat [1]. In aquatic environments, chromium predominantly exists in two oxidation states and as hexavalent (Cr(VI)) and trivalent (Cr(III)) chromium. Cr(III) serves as a vital nutrient for living beings and has a vital role in the metabolism of glucose, proteins, and fats [2]. In stark contrast, Cr(VI) has been unequivocally identified as a potent carcinogen and mutagen. Thus, it poses a serious threat to human health at even minimal concentrations. Cr(VI) and its associated compounds can infiltrate human bodies through contaminated drinking water, airborne particles, food, and even dust suspended in the air. The detrimental effects of Cr(VI) on DNA and gene expression can result in deteriorating health conditions such as nephritis, dermatosis, and various types of cancer. It can even have an adverse effect on the health of human offspring (International Agency for Research on Cancer, 2011). The Cr(VI) permissible limits in drinking water as per the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and Indian standard IS 10500 are 0.01 and 0.05 mg/L respectively. Consequently, the development of a precise and specific method for the sensing of Cr(VI) is of utmost importance.

To date, a variety of methods for the detection of Cr(VI) have been devised. Notable among these are atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS) [3], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), and X-ray fluorescence (XRF) [4]. However, the majority of these techniques have limitations as being time-consuming, intricate, and costly. Furthermore, they have not been successful in achieving the sensitive subjective differentiation among Cr(III) and Cr(VI). Consequently, there is a pressing need for the realization of a straightforward, cost-effective, quick, and reasonably sensitive responsive technique for the precise sensing of Cr(VI).

Carbon dots (CDs), an innovative addition to carbon nanomaterials domestic, have been garnering significant consideration due to their sturdy characteristics. These traits encompass multi-colored fluorescence, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, excellent solubility in water, and affordability in comparison to semiconductor quantum dots and commercially available dyes. CDs received extensive implementation in fields such as photocatalysis [5], environmental sensing [6], polymerization [7], ionic detection, bioimaging and drug delivery [8], and light-emitting diodes, among others [9]. Using alternate carbon sources or precursors, CDs can be achieved by employing various methods, such as top-down approaches namely arc discharge, laser ablation [10], chemical oxidation, and bottom-up approaches namely hydrothermal [6], microwave [11], and ultrasonic synthesis [12,13]. A wide variety of raw materials including inorganic materials [14], biological materials [15], and waste materials [16] can be opted. As a bottom-up approach, the green synthesis method employs natural carbon sources. It offers advantages in terms of being economical, reproducible, and environmentally friendly. This approach involves the replacing of expensive chemical-reducing agents with plant-derived entities, such as fruit juice [17], fruit peels [18], vegetables [19], plant leaves/extracts [20], or waste materials [21], for the hydrothermal carbonization based CDs synthesis.

Currently, limited investigations are devoted to the utility of green-synthesized CDs sensors for Cr(VI) detection. The primary detection principle in these studies is the effect of quenching that results from the differing linkage of the functional groups of CD's with the targeted Cr(VI) analyte. As an illustration, a research group [22] stated the green creation of luminescent CDs from the waste pulp of Carica papaya and applied them as label-free chemoprobes to monitor total Cr in fluids. The authors demonstrated a remarkable maximum detection limit (LOD) of 0.708 ppb. Similarly, another research group [23] reported a single-step environmentally friendly creation of CDs from groundnuts and employed them as a Cr(VI) sensor with 0.1 ppm LOD. Another research group [24] also contributed to this field by reporting the eco-friendly production of N and S co-doped biomass CDs via grapefruit juice and urea. The authors demonstrated sensitive Cr(VI) detection (LOD 0.155 μM). Jute plant (in concentrated H2SO4) synthesized CDs has been utilized for the colorimetric sensing of Cr(VI) (0.03 of μM LOD) [13]. Another research group [25] reported green synthesis of CDs from Tulsi leaves with a LOD of 4.5 ppb. However, till date, the utility of green CDs derived from fully developed green tea leaves (a waste product in the tea gardening industry) for chromium sensing in aqueous media has not been explored. Recently, only few literature refer to the deployment of tea leaves as a precursor of carbon for the creation of CDs, and none of these studies addressed Cr(VI) colorimetric sensing [26–29]. Thus, the novelty of the carried-out work in this article refers to the mature green tea leaves-based synthesis of CDs, which is not the case in the best related prior art that adopted tulsi leaves, grapefruit, groundnuts etc., which have herbal and other domestic applications. Thus, the desired objective that involved greater subjectivity towards waste to value added product development has been met in this article.

In this investigation, we present a hydrothermal technology-based production of water-soluble fluorescent CDs that exhibited a strong blue fluorescence. These were realized with mature green tea leaf powder as the only source of carbon. The synthesized CDs were thoroughly assessed with modern analyzation techniques such as ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) and Fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy, Field emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Thereafter, the findings were corroborated with the visual Cr(VI) identification through the combined mechanisms of high selectivity based inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching phenomena.

CDs were manufactured with mature and fresh green tea leaves (Camellia sinensis) as the exclusive carbon precursor. These leaves were collected from the tea garden stationed on the campus of the Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati. Various metal salts were employed in the study, including potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), copper sulfate (CuSO4), chromium sulfate (Cr2(SO4)3), sodium arsenite (NaAsO2), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), mercury chloride (HgCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), iron sulphide (FeSO4), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), and lithium nitrate (LiNO3). All these constituted a purity exceeding 99 %. The mentioned metal salts were procured from Merck, India. For all dilutions and the preparation of reagent solutions and bio-extracts, deionized (DI) water sourced from the Millipore Corporation grade aqua system (Milli-Q) was utilized.

CDs production involved the degradation of dried green tea leaves powder. This occurred through a hydrothermal process. The mature green tea leaves were first washed extensively with tap water, and then with DI water to remove marginally adhering dirt or washable impurities. Subsequently, the leaves were dehydrated at 37 °C for one day and were then ground into a fine powder with a grinder. Next, 1 g of this powdered material was contacted with DI water (50 mL) and swirled for 15 min to ensure the formation of a homogeneous mixture. This mixture was then shifted into a 100 mL capacity Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor and placed inside a muffle furnace for a duration of 10 h at 200 °C. Thereafter, the reactor was permitted to reach room temperature (RT). The resulting liquid, which had a brownish-yellow appearance, was subjected to centrifugation for 20 min at 14,000 rpm and was subsequently sieved through a membrane (0.22 μm) to eliminate bigger particles (Scheme 1 ). The filtered liquid was then employed for further characterization.

CDs derived from mature green tea leaves underwent a comprehensive characterization study. This was facilitated with various techniques. The UV–Vis spectrum of the CDs was captured by analyzing samples in a double-beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The spectra of fluorescence (FL) emission were captured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (model: LS 45, PerkinElmer, Singapore). Patterns for XRD (X-ray diffraction) were identified using Cu-Ka emitters at a wavelength of 1.5406 Å (SmartLab9KW, Rigaku, Japan). The FT-IR technique was employed to explore the pertinent functional groups of the CDs (Spectrum two, PerkinElmer, Singapore). Furthermore, the dimensions and structure of the CD samples were evaluated through a FETEM system (JEM 2100, JEOL electron microscope, Japan). To prepare the TEM grids, the 5 mL volume of a diluted and thoroughly sonicated sample solution was applied to a copper grid with a carbon coating, and the solution was evaporated completely at 25 °C. Eventually, XPS analyses were conducted with the X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Fisher Scientific Pvt. Ltd., UK).

In the context of sensing experiments, the initial step involved an examination of the pH effect across a broad pH range and reaction time. Thereafter, under the most favourable conditions, the sensing capabilities towards different heavy metal ions were assessed. The pH of the CDs was precisely altered from 2 to 8 and with either HCl and/or NaOH. The fluorescence intensity of this sample was examined to assess the influence of ambient conditions on the fluorescence stability of the CDs.

In the selectivity assessment procedures, 0.5 mL of a CDs solution (5.5 mg/mL) was contacted with 1.5 mL of individual metal ion solutions (Cr(VI), Cd(II), Pb(II), As(III), Cu(II), and Hg(II) at 10 mg/L and with a ratio of 1:3 (v/v). Thereby, the mixtures were thoroughly agitated. After allowing them to incubate at RT for 5 min, fluorescence spectra measurements were carried out with an excitation λ ex value of 360 nm and emission λ em value at 438 nm (5 nm slits). Also, visual observations were ensured to detect any alterations in the solution color. Notably, the most significant alterations in fluorescence intensity and the disappearance of color in the CDs solution were exclusively observed for an aqueous system that constituted Cr(VI) entities.

To assess upon the LOD for Cr(VI), spiked Cr(VI) DI water samples were introduced within the concentration range of 0.01–8.0 mg/L. These samples were evenly combined with CDs (1:3 (v/v) ratio) for 5 min. For this purpose, the same conditions mentioned earlier were followed. Any changes in color and spectral properties were closely monitored. This was primarily focused towards fluorescent intensities and spectral shifts. To establish the LOD, the fluorescence intensities F0 (system without metal ions) and F (system with metal ions) were utilized to construct the standard curve. This was achieved by plotting the graph between the fluorescence peak intensity of CDs (F0/F) and the concentration of Cr(VI). The resulting trend assured a linear relationship with a strong regression coefficient. The following equation was used to ascertain Cr(VI)'s LOD (Eq. (1)). In the above expression, ‘s’ represents the blank's standard deviation, and ‘k’ signifies the inclination of the calibration curve.
(1)
LOD = 3
s / k

The specificity of Cr(VI) ions was confirmed by adding 1.5 times the amount of various metal salts (including Hg(II), As(III), Cr(III), Fe(II), K+, Na+, Li2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), Ca2+, Ni(II)) containing solutions into the CDs solution. Following this, at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, each of these solutions’ fluorescence spectra was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The tap water was sourced from the Chemical Engineering Department of IIT Guwahati. It was subsequently utilized in its unfiltered state. Various concentrations of Cr(VI) ions ranging from 0.5 to 8 mg/L were introduced into the tap water prior to the agitation with the CDs. Subsequently, 360 nm was used as the excitation wavelength to measure their fluorescence spectra.

The synthesis of CDs is sensitively affected with the temperature of the process as the temperature being adopted for the synthesis influences the particle size of the CD. The temperature effect is primarily in terms of the breakage of several chemical bonds that exist in the cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose constituents of the dried tea leaves biomass. Furthermore, temperature facilitates the liberation of volatile substances and through the greater facilitation of hydrothermal reactions. Accordingly, the reactions govern the morphological and surface characteristics of the resultant CDs. According to a relevant prior art, the ideal synthesis conditions were inferred as 200 °C temperature and 10 h reaction duration [6]. The literature data summarized in Table S1 highlights that the CDs synthesized from tea leaves at lower synthesis temperatures (below 180 °C) tend to produce CDs with larger particle sizes (10 nm) [30,31]. Such a phenomenon can be attributed to the preservation of aggregated biomass containing cellulose fibers at lower synthesis temperatures.

Literature reports indicate that the CDs with small sizes (4.2 ± 0.825 nm and 2.32 nm, respectively) can be synthesized at a temperature range of 180–200 °C [26,28]. Also, with a prolonged reaction time of 10 h, it was possible to create CDs with partially retained crystallinity, a characteristic being known to enhance electrical conductivity [6]. However, enhancing the temperature beyond 200 °C could potentially lead to a reduction in CDs synthesis yield. This could result in the alteration of monosaccharides, oligosaccharides, organic acids present in the leafy biomass and into forms unsuitable for participation in the growth of CDs [6]. Considering this information in the available literature, to achieve smaller and more uniform CDs, a synthesis temperature of 200 °C was selected for the conducted investigations in the present study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic and microscopic analyses of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the structural and compositional aspects of the created CDs, the CDs were subjected to characterization with fluorescence and UV–Vis spectroscopy, FETEM, XRD, FTIR, and XPS, respectively.

A comprehensive exploration of the photoluminescence characteristics of CDs was conducted for variant process conditions. Accordingly, optical properties were assessed. The optical characteristics of CDs were assessed with both fluorescence and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 1 a) of CDs exhibited an elevation spectral absorption at 275 nm. This corroborates to the π-π* conversion of CC and conversion of CO to n-π*. These have been realized from components such as polyphenols, flavonoids, and related organic compounds of green tea [2,13,32,33]. The prepared solution of CDs displayed a yellowish hue under normal lighting conditions. However, they exhibited vivid blue fluorescence upon being subjected to excitation with UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm (inset of Fig. 1a and b). The fluorescence properties of the CDs were examined with distinct excitation wavelengths and within the range of 270–400 nm (Fig. 1b). With excitation wavelength enhancement from approximately 270-400 nm, a noticeable shift has been apparent towards longer wavelengths in the emission peak (ranging from 417 to 475 nm). Also, the intensity of CDs’ luminescence increased initially but declined later onwards. The highest emission intensity was detected at about 438 nm and for 360 nm excitation wavelength. Such a behaviour that ensures that the emissions being dependent upon the excitation wavelength, has been in good agreement with the reported findings [13].

The CDs particle size and morphological properties were characterized with FETEM, selected area electron diffraction (SAED), and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM). The observation indicates that the CDs have been evenly distributed and accordingly display a consistent spherical shape (Fig. 2 a). The size histogram exhibits a brief size range within the 4.2–6 nm range and possesses a mean dimension of approximately 5.2 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 2b). In HR-TEM (Fig. 2c), distinct lattice fringes of the CDs have been evident. They displayed a spacing of 0.216 nm and correspond to the (100) diffraction planes of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms (similar to those found in graphite). In the SAED image, as depicted in Fig. 2d, weak hexagonal electron diffraction points and a diffuse white ring have been observed, thereby conveying the amorphous character of the CDs [1,6,13].

The XRD pattern of the CDs reveals a very broad low-intensity peak centered at 2θ = 22.6° (Fig. 2e). This is associated with the (002) crystal plane of graphite. Thus, the trends convey that the CDs are amorphous in nature [6].

The CDs surface functional groups were subjected to analysis with the FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2f). In this spectrum, a strong and wide band at 3434 cm−1 has been evident and thereby ensured the presence of asymmetric stretching vibrations associated with –OH and –NH groups. Furthermore, the peak at 2067 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds. Notably, a pronounced vibration band at 1637 cm−1 can be corroborated by the prevalent amide bonds (OC–NH). Such a peak has been in agreement with the prior research findings [32]. Another peak around 1400 cm−1 indicates the existence of stretching vibrations associated with CC bonds. Additionally, a peak at 1050 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of –CO bonds. The bands observed in the 400 to 800 cm−1 range within the broader spectrum of CDs were attributed to flavonoid moieties. The FTIR spectrum aligns closely with previously reported profiles and features a prominent peak at approximately 685 cm−1. Thus, based on this work's findings and the information available in the literature, a conclusion can be drawn that the synthesized CDs contained a variety of hydrophilic functional groups, including hydroxyl (-OH), amine (-NH2), and carboxyl groups (-COOH) [32,34].

The XPS analysis was used to access both the elemental configuration and surface states of the CDs. Fig. 3 a represents CDs full scan XPS spectrum. Thereby, it conveyed three prominent peaks at 285.2, 399.2, and 531.2 eV. These resemble C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Fig. 3b displays the C1s spectrum, and revealed three discernible peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.0 eV. These convey the presence of CC, C–OH, and CO functional groups, respectively. The N1s spectrum in Fig. 3c illustrates peaks at 399.9 and 401.4 eV. These can be respectively ascribed to –CN and –NH functional groups. Lastly, Fig. 3d exhibits the O1s spectrum. In the spectrum, two peaks at 531.34 and 532.47 eV, correspond to CO and C–OH functional groups, respectively. In summary, the findings affirm the existence of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the surface of the CDs. Also, the findings align with the reported FTIR spectra [33].

The obtained CDs stability was based on a comprehensive examination that was undertaken under variant conditions, such as altered pH, NaCl concentration, storage duration, and exposure to UV irradiation. The impact of pH on the stability of CDs was examined, as pH is a recognized critical factor in influencing the sensing process. Both fluorescence intensity and quenched intensity of CDs are well-known to be pH-dependent. The pH levels of the CDs solution were adjusted within the pH range of 2–8 (with solutions of HCl and NaOH), and the findings have been detailed in Supplementary Information (SI) (Figs. S1a and SI). In an alkaline environment, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was notably weak. However, the CDs demonstrated strength and stability in an acidic setting. As shown in Fig. S1a (SI), the highest CDs fluorescence intensity remained relatively consistent within the pH range of 2–4.5. Beyond this range, a reduction in the CDs fluorescence intensity was detected. This suggests that the ideal pH range for effective sensing lies within the acidic range of 2–4.5. The CDs’ fluorescence behavior in relation to pH has been most likely attributed to the reversible protonation and deprotonation of the CDs in both alkaline and acidic conditions. At lower pH values, the robust interaction facilitated by hydrogen bonding among the –OH functional groups contribute to intense vibrational coupling. This assured broadened energy levels and increased structural rigidity of the CDs. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced. Conversely, the deprotonation process below alkaline conditions eliminate the effects of hydrogen bonding. This resulted in decreased vibrational coupling and a more discrete distribution of energy levels. Consequently, the CDs fluorescence intensity reduced [33,35]. As illustrated in Fig. S1b (SI), no noticeable alteration has been apparent in the intensity for enhanced NaCl concentration from 0 to 1 M. Such a behavior conveyed remarkable salt tolerance of the CDs and applicability in an environment that involves diverse ionic strength. As demonstrated in the supplementary information (Figs. S1c and SI), no noteworthy reduction has been apparent in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs even after a month of storage at 4 °C. For the case of extended storage durations, it is possible to freeze-dry the CDs solution to obtain a powdered form. Such a powdered form can be subsequently reconstituted in water prior to use. Furthermore, the photostability of the CDs was investigated through incessant exposure to UV light at 365 nm and for 30-min duration. The emissions from the CDs were monitored at various time intervals. Remarkably, no substantial alterations (as illustrated in Figs. S1d and SI) were observed in the emission intensity of the CDs, even after 30 min of uninterrupted UV irradiation. These are in agreement with the literature-reported trends [13,33].

To attain the maximal fluorescence quenching efficiency (expressed as F0/F, with F0 and F representing the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI) respectively) of Cr(VI) to CDs, various assessments were targeted and achieved to maximize key sensing parameters. These include the pH and incubation time of the detection system. The impact of pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) was examined by modifying the pH of the medium (pH range 2–8). Upon the introduction of Cr(VI), a discernible quenching of CDs fluorescence intensity occurred throughout the entire pH spectrum (depicted in Fig. S2a (SI)). Notably, it has been evident that the pH value of 4 offered the optimal conditions, and translated into the highest fluorescence quenching. Beyond such pH level, the efficiency of fluorescence quenching was notably reduced. Consequently, a pH of 4 was selected as the optimal aqueous medium for the sensitive sensing of Cr(VI).

The influence of incubation time was explored to determine the optimal duration required for the complexation between Cr(VI) and CDs (depicted in Figs. S2b and SI). With the introduction of 10 mg/L Cr(VI), it was evident that the CDs fluorescence intensity was nearly constant even after 5 min duration and even persisted for at least half an hour. With emphasis on a rapid and effective detection process, a 5-min incubation time was selected for the quenching of fluorescence. Consequently, the optimum ambiance parameters being selected for the subsequent experiments for Cr(VI) detection were a pH of 4 and an incubation time of 5 min.

The selectivity of the synthesized CDs was assessed by investigating the impact of different metal ions on the fluorescence quenching of CDs under identical experimental conditions. Fig. 4 a presents CDs fluorescence spectra upon exposure to various heavy metal ions, including Hg(II), Cd(II), Cr(VI), Cu(II), As(II), Ni(II), and Pb(II). Among the range of heavy metal ions examined, a distinct and significant fluorescence intensity decline was precisely detected for the Cr(VI) ions case. An intriguing observation is that upon the CDs exposure to UV light, the blue fluorescence of CDs altered to a colorless state for the Cr(VI) case due to fluorescence quenching. However, this has not been the case for the CDs exposed to other heavy metal ions (inset image of Fig. 4a). Thus, the CDs ensured the swift sensing of Cr(VI). In this work, it was observed that the as-prepared CDs fluorescence got quenched upon the introduction of Cr(VI).

The detection ability of CDs to Cr(VI) was assessed by introducing varying concentrations of Cr(VI) (ranging from 0.01 to 8 mg/L) and for optimized conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence response of CDs around the peak at 438 nm decreased progressively for increasing concentration of Cr(VI). Fig. 4c further displayed a strong linear correlation among F0/F and Cr(VI) concentration, and thereby ensured a high R2 (correlation coefficient) value of 0.99. The linear equation governing this relationship was found to be F0/F = 0.2101x + 1.0016. Based on this analysis, the LOD for CDs was obtained to be 0.004 mg/L (Eq. (1)). It is worth noting that the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and the Indian standard IS 10500 specified permissible limits of 0.01 mg/L and 0.05 mg/L for Cr(VI) in drinking water, respectively. Hence, the CDs demonstrated the practical applicability of the quantitative detection and determination of Cr(VI) ions. In this context, a comparative analysis of CDs for the detection of Cr(VI) with previously investigated nanomaterial probes has been presented in Table 1 . In accordance with prior studies, the CDs-based fluorescent probe exhibits appropriately superior sensitivity.

The assessment of the interference of other metal ions is an important aspect to judge upon the efficacy of the CDs sensor's response to fluorescence intensity. As depicted in Fig. 4d, no significant deviations were observed for the case in which various co-existing ions were present. This demonstrated the high selectivity of the CDs (as sensor probe) for Cr(VI) detection. Moreover, it was observed that for the case of 4 mg/L Cr(VI) concentration (resulting in a 50 % reduction in fluorescence intensity), and twice higher concentrations of other metal ions, no noteworthy alteration in the fluorescent signal of the CDs has been observed. This confirmed that the CDs exhibited high specificity and selectivity for Cr(VI) under the specified experimental conditions. Corroborating with the literature reported inference [13,37], the non-target ions did not interfere with the CD probe based accurate measurement of Cr(VI). This non-interference aspect has been attributed to the unique chemical and photo-physical properties of both CDs and Cr(VI). The associated mechanism is as follows. The Cr(VI) participate in inner filter effect and electron transfer mechanisms with CDs. However, such an effect does not occur with other metal ions. Accordingly, the Cr(VI) can act as a quencher through inner filter affect and the electron transfer mechanism occurs. This does not exist for other ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In these investigations, water from the lab tap was utilized to demonstrate the effective utilization of CDs for the Cr(VI) sensing in real-water specimens. Various concentrations of Cr(VI) (ranging from 0.5 to 8 mg/L) were introduced into the laboratory tap water samples, and the corresponding measurements were recorded (depicted in Fig. 5 a). The findings have been presented in Table S2 (SI). The determined recoveries of Cr(VI) ions, stretched from 96 to 103 %, and the data had an RSD (relative standard deviation) lower than 5 %. Therefore, it can be reasonably concluded that CDs serve as an excellent specialized nanoprobe for Cr(VI) detection in tap water specimens.

The addition of Cr(VI) resulted in a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. The potential reasons behind such a quenching phenomenon were explored. The quenching of CDs' fluorescence by Cr(VI) may have occurred primarily via the IFE (inner filter effect) and the mechanism of electron transfer. The IFE phenomenon often happens in the case in which a fluorescent substance's excitation or emission wavelengths considerably overlap with the quencher's absorption band. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the spectrum of Cr(VI) absorption in UV–Vis displayed three prominent peaks at 260, 359, and 440 nm. Simultaneously, the CDs displayed a peak (emission) at 438 nm for the 360 nm excitation wavelength. Notably, the excitation spectrum of CDs, with its peak at 360 nm, significantly coincided with Cr(VI) absorption spectra that reached its maximum at 359 nm (illustrated in Fig. 5b). The outcome revealed that Cr(VI) acted to shield a portion of the excitation light and also absorbed some of the emitted light generated by the CDs. Consequently, a significant IFE transpired among CDs and Cr(VI). Thereby, it adequately reduced/quenched the CDs' luminosity. Such an IFE-based sensing mechanism aligns well with findings from previous studies [13,41], and [37]. Such an IFE-based sensing probe demonstrates the potential for the delivery of rapid and accurate responses in the determination of Cr(VI) [2,13]. To elucidate the underlying mechanism of the CDs–Cr(VI) system, the FTIR analysis was conducted (Figs. S2c and SI) [1]. The addition of Cr(VI) lead to a significant reduction in the peak intensities. Thereby, it indicated that the fluorescence quenching was due to the interaction between Cr(VI) ions and the –CN, –NH, and –OH bonds that exist on the CDs surface.

The mature green tea leaves have been effective in synthesizing CDs (average particle size 5.2 ± 0.5 nm) following a hydrothermal process. The synthesized CDs exhibited a blue fluorescence emission at 438 nm for 360 nm excitation. Cr(VI) ions was found to be an excellent fluorescence quencher due to static quenching and IFE. Thereby, they have been employed as an efficient fluorescence sensor for the detection of Cr(VI). The sensing platform had a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 0.01–8 mg/L with a limit of detection of 0.004 mg/L. The sample recovery (85.5–101.6 %) was quite satisfactory with RSD of <1.4 % while testing the tap water sample. In summary, the demonstrated method offers a straightforward, selective, sensitive, economical, green, and unlabelled framework for the recognition of Cr(VI) ions. Accordingly, it strengthens the affirming of a fluorescence-based study platform for Cr(VI) detection. The reported findings hold promise for the precise and particular identification of Cr(VI) in actual specimens. The demonstrated nanosensor system exhibited greater synergy towards mentioned heavy metal sensing process and has versatile utility potential in fields related to both environment and biology.

Swagata Patra: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Animes K. Golder: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ramagopal V.S. Uppaluri: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thankfully acknowledge the Centre for the Environment, Department of Chemical Engineering, and Central Instruments Facility, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, India, for providing the necessary facilities for carrying out this research.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2024.115767.
